Service Recovery Initiatives:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola launched four named service recovery initiatives — Project Vistaar (Nov-2024 network transformation), Hyperservice (Q1 FY26, 1,000 mobile vans + AI diagnostics), HyperDelivery (same-day delivery), and Project Lakshya (OpEx cost restructuring) — which claimed 50% backlog reduction and 80% same-day resolution by Q3 FY26. However, these are self-reported and unverified metrics, and customer app rating remains at 3.2/5.0. Market share only recovered from a 9% trough to 16% — still far below the 47% peak. The fundamental constraint remains parts availability (60-75% vs 95% industry standard) and insufficient repair-capable service points (~300), which operational initiatives alone cannot fully resolve.

Service Recovery Initiatives

Ola Electric's service crisis — the single largest driver of its market share collapse from 47% to 8.6% — triggered a multi-pronged operational response beginning in late 2024. Four named initiatives were deployed in sequence, each targeting a different dimension of the service failure: network footprint, field service reach, delivery speed, and cost structure. Together they represent Ola's attempt to fix a broken D2C service model that had only ~300 repair-capable centers serving over 1M vehicles on road.

Project Vistaar (Network Transformation, Nov 2024)

Launched when Ola's market share had already fallen to ~24%, Project Vistaar was the first structural response to the service crisis. It aimed to optimize the retail and service footprint by converting pure-sales experience centers into combined sales-and-service outlets. At launch, only ~300 of 900 experience centers had repair capability, meaning 67% were showrooms with no service function. Vistaar was designed to close this gap, reducing average delivery time from 12 days to 3-4 days at select locations. However, the initiative was reactive — it came after the CCPA show-cause notice (Oct 2024) had already triggered mass consumer defection, and the structural constraint of centralized parts supply was not addressed.

Hyperservice (Q1 FY26)

The most aggressive service recovery measure, Hyperservice deployed 1,000 mobile service vans across India, equipped with AI diagnostics for remote troubleshooting. The initiative claimed to reduce the service backlog by 50% by Q3 FY26 and achieve 80% same-day resolution — a dramatic improvement from the 7-15 day turnaround at the crisis peak. AI diagnostics enabled technicians to identify issues remotely before dispatching the correct parts, reducing repeat visits. Operational data showed service turnaround time improved from 7-15 days (peak) to 3-4 days (post-Hyperservice), though this remains 2-3x worse than the 1-2 day industry standard maintained by Bajaj (Rs 400/visit), TVS, and Hero.

HyperDelivery (Same-Day Delivery)

HyperDelivery introduced same-day delivery capability at select experience centers, aiming to improve the purchase experience. In an environment where Ola's primary competitive weakness was post-purchase service, faster delivery was a lever to improve the initial customer experience. The initiative was operationally sound but strategically limited — faster delivery does not address the trust deficit that discourages customers from making the purchase decision in the first place.

Project Lakshya (Cost Optimization)

Project Lakshya targeted the cost side of the crisis: OpEx was slashed from Rs 840 Cr (Q4 FY25 peak) to Rs 484 Cr (Q3 FY26) — a 42% reduction in two quarters. Thousands of sales staff were let go, underperforming experience centers were closed, and marketing spend was cut drastically. This restructuring lowered the EBITDA breakeven threshold from ~40,000 units/month to ~15,000 units/month. While essential for survival, the cost cuts reduced Ola's sales capacity exactly when it needed to rebuild volume, creating a tension between financial survival and growth recovery.

Effectiveness Assessment

The recovery initiatives show mixed results. Operationally, service metrics improved: backlog reduced, turnaround times compressed, and market share stabilized at ~16% (Q4 FY26) from the 8.6% trough (Oct 2025). Financially, the combined effect of cost restructuring and gross margin improvement (12% to 36%) drove EBITDA positive for the first time (+2.1% in Q3 FY26, +4.5% in Q4 FY26). However, commercially, sales have not recovered to sustainable levels: Q4 FY26 monthly average of ~13,000 units remains below the 15,000 breakeven threshold, and the 39K quarterly units is a fraction of the 108K peak.

The core issue remains structural: parts availability at 60-75% (vs 95% industry standard) and only ~300 repair-capable service points cannot be fully addressed by mobile vans and AI diagnostics. Legacy players with 1,000+ full-service points and 30-40 year parts supply chains maintain an infrastructure advantage that operational initiatives cannot quickly close.

Key Data Points

  • Project Vistaar: launched Nov 2024 at ~24% market share, targeted network transformation
  • Hyperservice: 1,000 mobile service vans deployed Q1 FY26
  • AI diagnostics enabled remote troubleshooting and parts pre-dispatch
  • Claimed 50% backlog reduction by Q3 FY26 (self-reported, unverified)
  • Claimed 80% same-day resolution (self-reported, unverified)
  • Turnaround time improved from 7-15 days (peak) to 3-4 days (post-Hyperservice)
  • Industry standard turnaround: 1-2 days (Bajaj, TVS, Hero)
  • Parts availability: 60-75% (Ola) vs 95% (industry standard)
  • Project Lakshya: OpEx reduced from Rs 840 Cr to Rs 484 Cr (-42%)
  • EBITDA breakeven lowered from ~40K to ~15K units/month
  • App rating remained at 3.2/5.0 despite operational improvements
  • Market share recovered from 8.6% (Oct 2025 trough) to 15.95% (Mar 2026)
  • Q4 FY26 sales: 39K units vs 108K peak quarterly volume

---

Backward Integration:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Controlling the full value chain from raw materials to distribution is Ola's primary moat, but the Gigafactory only reached cell cost parity with import prices ($80/kWh) in April 2026 — meaning the first 2 years of production were more expensive than buying from Chinese suppliers.

Backward Integration Strategy

Core Thesis

Control the full value chain from raw materials → cells → battery packs → vehicles → distribution to achieve cost leadership and supply security.

---

Key Components

1. Cell Manufacturing (Highest Impact)

  • Battery = 35% of BOM for Ola S1 Pro
  • 4680 form factor cells being developed in-house
  • Ola Gigafactory: Phase 1(a) 1.4 GWh → Phase 1(b) 5 GWh → Target 20 GWh
  • Currently importing cells with no long-term contracts >1 year
  • In-house manufacturing eliminates import dependency, forex exposure, and supplier margin

2. In-House Component Manufacturing

Already manufacturing at Futurefactory:
  • Battery packs
  • Motors (since Aug 2023)
  • Vehicle frames (since Aug 2023)
  • Electronics and power modules
  • BMS (Battery Management System)

3. Co-Located Suppliers

  • Building supplier park within EV hub
  • Reduces logistics costs, improves JIT delivery, enables quality control
  • Part of the SIPCOT-leased 417.59 acres

4. Raw Material Security

  • Evaluating long-term supply agreements for lithium, nickel, cobalt, manganese
  • Assessing direct/indirect ownership of processing facilities

---

Financial Impact

| Lever | Current State | Target State |
|-------|---------------|--------------|
| Cell cost | Imported, ~35% BOM | In-house, cost reduced |
| BOM/Revenue | 89.6% (Q1 FY24) | Declining with vertical integration |
| Supply security | No contracts >1yr | Long-term + in-house |
| Gross margin | 13.2% (Q1 FY24) | Target 20%+ |

---

Product_Mix_Cannibalization:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
S1 X cannibalization is a red herring — gross profit per unit actually INCREASED 26% (₹24,300 → ₹30,527) despite ASP falling 34%, because Gigafactory cells and the simpler S1 X BOM collapsed COGS faster than the selling price declined.

Ola Electric: Product Mix & S1 X Cannibalization Deep-Dive

The Product Portfolio (as of May 2026)

Current Lineup

| Model | Segment | Price Range (₹) | Battery | Range (km) | Launch | |-------|---------|-----------------|---------|------------|--------| | S1 Pro | Premium | 1,30,000-1,50,000 | 3.97 kWh | 170 | Sep 2021 | | S1 Air | Mid-range | 1,10,000-1,20,000 | 3 kWh | 120 | Aug 2023 | | S1 X (2 kWh) | Mass-market | 69,999 | 2 kWh | 75 | Sep 2023 | | S1 X (3 kWh) | Mass-market | 89,999 | 3 kWh | 115 | Sep 2023 | | S1 X (4 kWh) | Mass-market | 1,05,000 | 4 kWh | 150 | Sep 2023 | | S1 X+ | Mass-market+ | 79,999 | 3 kWh | 120 | Late 2024 | | Gen 3 S1 | Next-gen | ~89,000 | 3.97 kWh | 150 | Mar 2025 | | Roadster | Motorcycle | 74,999-1,04,999 | 3.5-6 kWh | 200+ | FY26 |

The S1 X Volume Takeover

FY25 Official Numbers (from Ola Electric's filings)

  • Total FY25 deliveries: 359,221 units
  • S1 X deliveries: 196,123 units → 54.6% of total volume
  • Remaining (S1 Pro + S1 Air): 163,098 units → 45.4% of volume

Estimated Quarterly Product Mix Evolution

| Quarter | S1 Pro % | S1 Air % | S1 X % | Blended ASP (₹) | Revenue Impact | |---------|---------|---------|--------|-----------------|----------------| | Q1 FY25 | 35% | 10% | 15% | 1,35,000 | High ASP, peak revenue | | Q2 FY25 | 28% | 12% | 28% | 1,22,000 | S1 X gaining traction | | Q3 FY25 | 18% | 10% | 45% | 1,10,000 | S1 X becomes dominant | | Q4 FY25 | 12% | 8% | 60% | 98,000 | S1 X = majority of sales | | Q1 FY26 | 10% | 7% | 65% | 95,000 | Volume collapse begins | | Q2 FY26 | 8% | 5% | 70% | 92,000 | Premium buyers gone | | Q3 FY26 | 6% | 4% | 75% | 89,000 | Near-complete S1 X dominance | | Q4 FY26 | 7% | 5% | 72% | 90,000 | Slight recovery in mix |

Note: Ola does not officially disclose model-wise quarterly splits. Above estimates derived from VAHAN registration data cross-referenced with analyst reports and company disclosures.

The Cannibalization Paradox

What Looks Bad (Revenue Side)

  • ASP dropped 34%: from ₹1.35L (Q1 FY25) to ₹0.89L (Q3 FY26)
  • Revenue per unit crashed, making the top-line shrink faster than volume
  • S1 X at ₹69,999-89,999 competes with ICE scooters, dragging down premium perception

What's Actually Good (Margin Side)

  • S1 X has a simpler BOM — fewer components, cheaper assembly
  • Gen 3 platform reduced manufacturing complexity
  • Gigafactory LFP cells cut battery costs by ~30%
  • Result: 34.3% gross margin on lower ASP (better than 18% margin on higher ASP)

The Math

| Scenario | ASP (₹) | Gross Margin (%) | Gross Profit per Unit (₹) | |----------|---------|------------------|--------------------------| | Q1 FY25 (S1 Pro heavy) | 1,35,000 | ~18% | ~24,300 | | Q3 FY26 (S1 X heavy + Gigafactory) | 89,000 | ~34.3% | ~30,527 |

Gross profit per unit actually INCREASED by 26% despite ASP falling 34%. The S1 X is not killing margins — it's improving them, but only because the Gigafactory slashed input costs.

Strategic Implication

The S1 X "cannibalization" is a red herring. The real problem is: 1. Trust deficit → fewer total buyers (164K vs 359K) 2. NOT bad unit economics → margins are actually record-high

If Ola can rebuild trust and get back to 30,000+ units/month at current S1 X mix + Gigafactory costs, they would be solidly EBITDA-positive.

Competitor ASP Comparison (FY26)

| Company | Avg Price Range (₹) | Volume (units) | Revenue/Unit (₹) | |---------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------| | Ola Electric | 69,999-1,50,000 | 164,000 | ~89,000 (blended) | | Ather Energy | 1,09,000-1,55,000 | 263,000 | ~1,39,000 | | TVS iQube | 1,00,000-1,80,000 | 371,000 | ~1,27,000 | | Bajaj Chetak | 1,15,000-1,57,000 | 289,000 | ~1,38,000 | | Hero Vida | 89,000-1,40,000 | 150,000 | ~1,00,000 |

Ola has the lowest blended ASP because S1 X dominates its mix. Every other player sells primarily in the ₹1L-1.5L band.

B2B_Battery_Pivot:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola's 6 GWh Gigafactory is operating at ~16% utilization (6 GWh built, ~1 GWh consumed internally), forcing a B2B pivot via Ola Shakti (stationary storage) and OEM cell supply. But as of May 2026, zero confirmed B2B orders or revenue exist — 100% of Q4 FY26 sales remain E2W. The pivot faces a 900 GWh global cell glut where Chinese manufacturers dominate at the same $80/kWh cost. The data explicitly warns: "bad if selling B2B."

B2B Battery Pivot

---

The Utilization Crisis

Ola's Gigafactory was sized for an optimistic demand scenario that collapsed:

  • Installed capacity: 6.0 GWh (enough for ~1.5M+ scooters/year at 4 kWh average)
  • Current demand (own scooters): ~0.4-0.6 GWh/year (39K units/quarter at ~3.5 kWh average)
  • Estimated utilization: ~16%
  • Idle capacity: ~5 GWh — a massive stranded asset risk
  • Capex invested: ~Rs 4,000 Cr (FY24-FY25)
  • Annual depreciation: Rs 566 Cr (Mar-25) — scaling with gigafactory asset base
  • Annual interest cost: Rs 366 Cr (Mar-25)

---

The Two B2B Channels

1. Ola Shakti (Stationary Energy Storage)

Ola has announced stationary energy storage solutions under the "Ola Shakti" brand. This targets commercial and industrial backup power, grid storage, and rural energy access. The LFP chemistry's thermal stability and long cycle life make it suitable for stationary applications.

2. OEM Cell Supply

Ola aims to sell cells to other vehicle manufacturers — including potentially to competitors in the E2W, 3W, and light EV segments. The pitch is domestic supply chain security vs imported Chinese cells.

Zero Confirmed Execution

Despite the Q4 FY26 management focus explicitly shifting to "B2B Battery Supply & Margin Defense," there are no confirmed B2B orders, contracts, or revenue in any financial statement. The pivot is a stated intent with zero execution evidence.

---

The Global Overcapacity Headwind

The B2B cell market is saturated:

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Global cell manufacturing capacity | 4,200 GWh |
| Excess supply | ~900 GWh (21% overhang) |
| Chinese LFP pack price (2026) | $80/kWh |
| Ola LFP 46100 cost (Apr-2026) | $80/kWh (parity) |
| Key Chinese competitors | CATL, BYD — decades of scale advantage |
| Chinese-backed players in Africa | Spiro (Kenya), MAX (Nigeria) |

BATTERY_SUPPLY_CHAIN explicitly warns against selling B2B into a 900 GWh glut: "good for Ola if buying; bad if selling B2B." Ola would enter a market where Chinese manufacturers have:


  • 4,200 GWh of domestic capacity enabling unparalleled economies of scale

  • ~900 GWh excess supply enabling aggressive market-share-buying pricing

  • Established African distribution already deployed

---

The Hidden Connection: Vehicle-Level Margin is the Real Play

Ola cannot win on cell sales (zero cost advantage at $80/kWh parity with China). But it can bypass cell commoditization by selling value-added battery packs integrated into vehicles where the 30% BOM reduction provides vehicle-level margin. Battery-as-a-Service (BaaS) subscription revenue is the monetization model that could work — but only if Ola solves service infrastructure first. The export escape hatch ($2-3B TAM by 2028) is the same logic: sell vehicles, not cells.

---

Ola's B2B pivot includes: (1) Ola Shakti residential home battery storage system, and (2) cell supply to other automakers. This follows the CATL/BYD model of becoming a merchant cell supplier. The hedge works in both directions: if scooter sales recover, in-house cells improve margins; if not, cell supply becomes the core business.
Context: Section 4: Gigafactory & Technology Pivot — B2B pivot

Connections

### Causes

- Gigafactory overcapacity (6 GWh, ~16% utilization) — forced by scooter sales collapse

- Market share collapse (55.81% -> 15.95%) — domestic demand insufficient to absorb capacity

- Service crisis destroyed trust and volume — preventing organic demand growth

### Effects

- Stranded asset risk: 90% of 6 GWh capacity idle, Rs 4,000+ Cr capex at risk

- If B2B fails AND scooters don't recover: the gigafactory becomes a stranded asset

- Depreciation (Rs 566 Cr/yr) and interest (Rs 366 Cr/yr) are fixed costs that don't reduce with utilization

- B2B pivot may require further investment in sales channels, certification, and customer support

### Amplifiers

- 900 GWh global excess cell supply suppresses B2B pricing — same $80/kWh as Ola's cost

- Chinese OEMs have decades of relationship advantage in global B2B markets

- No confirmed orders as of May 2026 — pivot is all promise, no proof

- Phase 2 (12 GWh, Jul 2027) and full build-out (20 GWh, Mar 2028) would worsen the mismatch

### Linked Nodes

- Technology/Gigafactory_Updates

- Technology/LFP_46100_Cell

- Market/Export_Markets_Detail

- Opportunities/Export Markets

- Opportunities/Cell Manufacturing

- Financial/Balance Sheet Analysis

- Risks/Gigafactory Execution

- Market/Battery_Supply_Chain
Operating Leverage:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Revenue scaling from ₹373 Cr to ₹5,010 Cr drove fixed-cost absorption and transformed gross margins from -5.4% to 13.2%, but the reverse is now true: with FY25 revenue declining 9.9% and Futurefactory under-utilised at 1M capacity, fixed costs are crushing profitability.

Operating Leverage Gains

---

The Trend

| Metric | FY22 | FY23 | Q1 FY24 |
|--------|------|------|---------|
| Revenue (₹ Cr) | 373 | 2,631 | 1,243 |
| Employee Cost / Revenue | 61.9% | 15.3% | 7.4% |
| Ad Spend / Revenue | 10.8% | — | 1.0% |
| R&D Spend / Revenue | 38.5% | 18.3% | 7.3% |
| Asset Turnover | 0.34 | — | 2.68 (ann.) |
| Adj. Gross Margin | -5.4% | 7.6% | 13.2% |

---

What's Driving It

1. Fixed Cost Absorption

As revenue scaled from ₹373 Cr → ₹5,010 Cr, fixed costs (factory, corporate, R&D infrastructure) were spread across more units.

2. Gen 2 Platform Efficiency

68.85% component commonality reduces:
  • Manufacturing complexity
  • Inventory requirements
  • Supplier management overhead
  • Per-unit R&D amortization

3. In-House Manufacturing

  • Battery packs, motors, frames made in-house
  • Eliminates supplier margins on key components
  • Started motor + frame manufacturing Aug 2023

4. D2C Efficiency

  • Brought experience centres in-house (Jul 2023)
  • Eliminated ₹700 Cr+ annual fee to Ola Fleet Technologies
  • Digital-first model reduces per-sale distribution cost

---

The Problem

Operating leverage works in both directions. With FY25 revenue declining 9.9%, fixed costs become a burden again:


  • Factory under-utilization (1M capacity, lower volumes)

  • R&D spend must continue regardless of sales

  • Experience centre costs are largely fixed

---

Data Sources

DATA: Source: QUARTERLY_FINANCIALS_EXT.csv
EBITDA margins swung from -22.1% (Q4 FY25 trough) to +4.5% (Q4 FY26) as OpEx was slashed 42% (₹840 Cr → ₹484 Cr) — the breakeven dropped from ~40K to ~15K units/month.
DATA: Source: PRODUCT_MIX_QUARTERLY.csv
Gross margin more than doubled from 18% (Q1 FY25) to 36% (Q4 FY26) even as blended ASP fell 33% — Gen 2 platform (68.85% commonality) + Gigafactory cells drove the improvement.
DATA: Source: E2W_MONTHLY_REGISTRATIONS.csv
Monthly registrations collapsed from 53,320 (Mar 24) to 7,567 (Nov 25) — the 1M-unit factory capacity was sized for a demand level that disappeared after the service crisis.
DATA: Source: GIGAFACTORY_MILESTONES.csv
Gigafactory at 6 GWh with only ~16% utilisation (39K units/quarter consumes ~0.5 GWh). Each kWh carries disproportionate depreciation (₹566 Cr/yr, Mar 25 P&L) and interest (₹366 Cr/yr).
DATA: Source: PROFIT_AND_LOSS.csv
Ola's selling and admin expenses ballooned from Rs 58 Cr (FY21) to Rs 1,598 Cr (FY25) — a 27.5x increase in 4 years — while sales grew from Rs 0.86 Cr to Rs 4,514 Cr. The SG&A-to-sales ratio deteriorated from 67:1 (FY21) to 35% of revenue (FY25). This cost structure was built for the 50%+ market share era and became unsustainable when share collapsed.
Causal chain: High-growth spending→fixed cost build-up→volume collapse→SG&A unsustainable→restructuring required→breakeven lowered→extreme cost cuts implemented
Cells: Row 2 Sales: Mar-21=0.86, Mar-25=4514; Row 7 Selling and admin: Mar-21=58.06, Mar-25=1598
DATA: Source: PROFIT_AND_LOSS.csv
Ola's employee cost grew from Rs 197 Cr (FY21) to Rs 465 Cr (FY25) — only 136% growth, far slower than revenue growth (5,248x), demonstrating operating leverage in fixed costs. As % of sales, it dropped from 22,935% to 10.3%. Despite 56% revenue decline from peak, employee base was not significantly reduced, suggesting retention of Gigafactory and R&D talent.
Causal chain: Fixed employee base was leveraged across growing sales, dramatically reducing employee cost as % of revenue; strategic retention of Gigafactory talent even during crisis
Cells: Row 6 Employee Cost: Mar-21=197.25, Mar-23=429.72, Mar-24=450.6, Mar-25=465
DATA: Source: Ola_after_ipo.txt
OpEx was slashed from Rs 840 Cr (Q4 FY25 peak) to Rs 484 Cr (Q3 FY26) — a 42% reduction in two quarters. Achieved by firing thousands of sales staff, closing underperforming experience centers, and cutting marketing spend drastically. This is what drove the EBITDA breakeven threshold from 40K to 15K units/month.
Causal chain: The drastic OpEx reduction was a survival move: revenue had collapsed and costs had to follow; this drove the EBITDA breakeven threshold from 40K to 15K units/month
Cells: Section 3: Cost cutting details
DATA: Source: QUARTERLY_FINANCIALS_EXT.csv
Mgmt Primary Focus shifted dramatically across 8 quarters: Q1 FY25 'Scale and Market Dominance' → Q2 'IPO Roadshow' → Q3 'Defending Price Wars' → Q4 'Crisis Management & Hyperservice' → Q1 FY26 'Cost Cutting & OpEx Reduction' → Q3 FY26 'Structural Profitability & LFP Pivot' → Q4 FY26 'B2B Battery Supply & Margin Defense'. A complete strategy reset from aggressive growth to survival.
Causal chain: Management focus went from aggressive growth to survival to restructuring to profitability to B2B diversification in just 8 quarters — a complete strategy reset

D2C Omnichannel:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola's D2C network of 935 Experience Centres eliminated dealer margins (8-12% in auto) and saved ₹700+ Cr annually in selling fees, but the thin service network (~300 repair-capable centres) became a catastrophic bottleneck — 7-15 day turnaround at peak vs 1-2 days for legacy competitors.

D2C Omnichannel Distribution Network

---

Network Scale (as of Oct 2023)

| Component | Count |
|-----------|-------|
| Experience Centres | 935 |
| Service Centres | 414 (410 within experience centres) |
| Coverage | Pan-India |
| Rank | India's largest company-owned EV experience centre network |

---

How It Works

Digital-First Sales Journey

1. Discovery — Ola Electric website / app 2. Experience — Visit experience centre (see, touch, test ride) 3. Purchase — Online booking via website 4. Financing — Through Ola Financial Services + 12 partner institutions (up to 5-year loans) 5. Delivery — Direct to customer with same-day registration 6. After-sales — Service booking via Ola Electric Companion app

---

Strategic Rationale

Why D2C?

1. Customer relationship — Direct data on preferences, behavior, feedback 2. Cost efficiency — No dealer margins (typically 8-12% in auto) 3. Pricing control — Uniform pan-India pricing, no dealer negotiation 4. Brand experience — Controlled environment, consistent messaging

Previous Model (until Jul 2023)

  • Experience centres operated by Ola Fleet Technologies (group company)
  • Ola Electric paid selling/distribution fees: ₹722.6 Cr (FY23)
  • From Jul 2023: Operations brought in-house → eliminates ₹700 Cr+ annual fee

---

Competitive Comparison

| Dimension | Ola D2C | TVS/Bajaj/Hero Dealers |
|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| Touchpoints | 935 | 4,000-7,000+ |
| Cost structure | Company-owned (higher fixed, lower variable) | Franchisee (variable) |
| Customer data | Full visibility | Limited/dealer-mediated |
| Brand experience | Controlled | Variable by dealer |
| Scale | Limited, needs capital to expand | Already at scale |
| Trust | New relationship | Multi-decade relationships |

---

Data Sources

DATA: Source: SERVICE_NETWORK_COMPARISON.csv
Ola's ~300 repair-capable centres vs TVS 1,000+ and Bajaj 1,480+ dealers. Parts availability: Ola 60-75% vs Bajaj/TVS 95%. Turnaround: Ola 7-15 days vs TVS/Bajaj 1-2 days. Service cost: Ola ₹800-1,500 vs Bajaj ₹400.
DATA: Source: QUARTERLY_FINANCIALS_EXT.csv
Ola eliminated ₹722.6 Cr annual selling/distribution fee (FY23) by bringing Experience Centres in-house from Jul 2023 — a structural cost saving that enabled operating leverage gains.
DATA: Source: EV_SEGMENT_COMPARISON.csv
Ola's FY26 revenue/unit ₹223,902 is the highest in the industry (TVS ₹126,686, Bajaj ₹138,408), reflecting the fixed-cost burden of company-owned D2C vs legacy franchisee models at scale.
DATA: Source: CUSTOMER_SENTIMENT.csv
Google Play Store rating declined from 4.0 (launch) to 3.2 (May 2026) vs TVS iQube 4.2 and Ather 4.0 — the D2C digital touchpoint became a net negative for purchase conversion.
DATA: Source: market/SERVICE_NETWORK_COMPARISON.csv
Legacy players (Hero, Bajaj, TVS) all offer hybrid mechanic access — customers can get their EVs serviced at any independent local mechanic. Ola and Ather use proprietary service networks, creating a coverage disadvantage in smaller towns. India's 2W market depends on 50,000+ independent mechanics for service.
Causal chain: India's 2-wheeler market depends on 50,000+ independent mechanics for service. Legacy OEMs leverage decades of mechanic training networks. Pure-play EVs lack this ecosystem, limiting reach to Tier 3+ towns
Cells: Row 2-4 (Hero/Bajaj/TVS): Independent_Mechanic_Access = Yes (hybrid); Row 5-6 (Ather/Ola): Independent_Mechanic_Access = No (proprietary)
DATA: Source: SERVICE_CRISIS_TIMELINE.csv
Hyperservice deployment (Q1 FY26): 1,000 service vans + AI diagnostics deployed. Claimed 50% backlog reduction and 80% same-day resolution by Q3 FY26. However, these are self-reported metrics not independently verified, and customer app rating remains at 3.2/5.0. The gap between claimed and perceived improvement is significant.
Causal chain: Hyperservice deployment→operational metrics improve→self-reported success→customer perception lags→app rating still low→trust recovery takes longer than operational recovery
Cells: SERVICE_CRISIS Row 11 Hyperservice deployed Q1-FY26; Row 12 'Service backlog reduced 50% - 80% same-day resolution claimed' Q3-FY26
DATA: Source: Ola_after_ipo.txt
TVS had 1,000+ existing dealer-service centers before entering EVs. Bajaj had 3,800+ touchpoints. Hero had the largest dealer network in India. Ola had ~900 experience centers with limited service capability. When scooters failed, customers had nowhere to go — the D2C model lacked the service density of legacy dealer networks.
Causal chain: Ola's D2C model lacked the service density of legacy OEM dealer networks. This structural disadvantage became fatal when product quality issues emerged
Cells: Section 2: 'Why legacy players won' paragraph
DATA: Source: CUSTOMER_SENTIMENT.csv
Ola's service cost at Rs 800-1,500 per visit is the highest in the industry — double Bajaj's Rs 400. Combined with 7-15 day turnaround (vs 1-2 for Bajaj/TVS/Hero), Ola offers the worst service economics in the E2W industry despite having only 3 years of service experience vs 30-40+ for legacy players.
Causal chain: No legacy service network→higher per-visit cost→longer turnaround→worst TCO for consumer→brand switching→market share loss

Ola acts as "both EV manufacturer and dealer in India" — a model "relatively new and unproven in India's automobile industry" (Redseer Report). Ola had 935 experience centres as of Oct 31, 2023, majority open less than one year.

Ola paid selling and distribution fees to OFTP. Ola assumed control on July 1, 2023. Prior experience distributing through these centres was limited.

Pre-IPO risk that materialized post-IPO: "Inadequate sales and service performance could negatively impact our reputation and brand." "Failure to deliver a superior sales service through our experience centres could weaken our competitive advantage." Ola plans to use third-party dealers internationally where it cannot control them directly.

D2C model eliminates dealership margins (Redseer).

EV Hub Tamil Nadu:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
The 417.59-acre SIPCOT-leased EV Hub in Krishnagiri concentrates Ola's entire manufacturing, R&D, and supplier ecosystem in one geography — creating powerful cost synergies via co-location but also exposing the entire company to single-location execution risk.

EV Hub Tamil Nadu

---

Physical Scale

| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| Location | Krishnagiri & Dharmapuri districts, Tamil Nadu |
| Land Area | 417.59 acres |
| Land Provider | SIPCOT (State Industries Promotion Corp of TN) |
| Lease Terms | Subject to minimum investment + employment commitments |

---

Hub Components

1. Ola Futurefactory — E2W manufacturing, 1M units/year installed capacity
2. Ola Gigafactory — Cell manufacturing (under construction)
3. Co-located Supplier Park — Integrated supply chain within the hub
4. R&D / BIC — Battery Innovation Centre for cell technology development

---

Tamil Nadu Incentives Package

| Incentive | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| SGST Reimbursement | 100% for 20 years on EVs sold + registered in TN |
| Investment Promotion Subsidy | Capital assistance |
| GST on Capital Goods | Incentive in inverted tax situations |
| Land Cost Subsidy | Concessional SIPCOT lease rates |
| Electricity Tax Incentive | Reduced power cost for manufacturing |
| Corporate Tax Rate | 15% for new manufacturing companies |
| Recognized in FY23 | ₹204.6 Cr capital + revenue assistance + ₹69.2 Cr interest income on govt grant |

---

Strategic Importance

1. Integrated ecosystem — Manufacturing, supply chain, and R&D co-located
2. Cost advantages — State incentives + logistics savings from co-located suppliers
3. Single location risk — All manufacturing concentrated in one geography
4. MOU obligations — Must meet minimum investment and employment targets or face penalties/lease cancellation

---

Data Sources

DATA: Source: GIGAFACTORY_MILESTONES.csv
Ola Gigafactory scaled from 0 GWh (Mar 2024) to 6.0 GWh (Apr 2026) at the hub, with LFP 46100 cells at $80/kWh delivering 30% BOM reduction — from $135/kWh at announcement.
DATA: Source: POLICY_REGULATORY_TIMELINE.csv
Tamil Nadu incentives: 100% SGST reimbursement for 20 years on EVs sold + registered in TN, 15% corporate tax rate for new manufacturing companies, and ₹204.6 Cr recognised in FY23 as capital and revenue assistance.
DATA: Source: EV_SEGMENT_COMPARISON.csv
Ola's FY26 gross margin recovered to 36% (Q4 FY26) as in-house cell production at the hub reduced BOM — from 12% trough (Q4 FY25) when still importing cells.
DATA: Source: E2W_MONTHLY_REGISTRATIONS.csv
Ola registrations recovered from 7,567 (Nov 25 trough) to 23,713 (Mar 26), supported by TN's continued EV incentives through Dec 2027 — including ₹10,000-20,000 per vehicle subsidy + 100% road tax exemption.
DATA: Source: POLICY_REGULATORY_TIMELINE.csv
Tamil Nadu EV Policy (2019-Dec 2027) offers Rs 10,000-20,000 per E2W with 100% road tax + registration exemption. Ola's Krishnagiri factory is in Tamil Nadu, giving it home state policy advantage — double the central subsidy level. This partially offsets Karnataka's adverse tax change (ended 100% road tax exemption Apr-2026).
Causal chain: Tamil Nadu policy→Ola factory benefit→production cost advantage→partially offsets Karnataka negative→policy geography matters for manufacturing location decisions
Cells: Row 12 Tamil Nadu EV Policy: E2W_Subsidy_Per_Vehicle='Rs 10000-20000 per E2W', Key_Conditions='100% road tax + registration exempt'
DATA: Source: financial_mda.txt
Ola Futurefactory installed capacity: 1 million units per year as of Oct 2023. FY25 sales were ~340K units (34% utilization). FY26 sales ~164K units (16.4% utilization). The 1M unit factory running at 16-34% utilization means massive fixed cost under-absorption, making each unit more expensive to produce.
Causal chain: 1M unit factory running at 16-34% utilization means massive fixed cost under-absorption, making each unit more expensive to produce and compressing margins
Cells: financial_mda Page 312 (capacity); Ola_after_ipo Section 1 (FY25/FY26 units)
DATA: Source: POLICY_REGULATORY_TIMELINE.csv
Maharashtra EV Policy (2025-2030) offers Rs 10,000 per E2W with 100% road tax + registration exemption and toll waivers — double the central PM E-DRIVE subsidy. This makes Maharashtra (Mumbai/Pune) one of the most attractive E2W markets. Ola's Krishnagiri factory can serve this market but needs logistics infrastructure.
Causal chain: Maharashtra double subsidy→strong urban market→critical for Ola recovery→service quality must match→to capture policy benefit
Cells: Row 10 Maharashtra EV Policy: E2W_Subsidy_Per_Vehicle='Rs 10000 per E2W', Key_Conditions='100% road tax + registration exempt; toll waivers'
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Ola built the Futurefactory in eight months, reaching installed capacity of 1 million units per year as of October 31, 2023. It is the largest integrated and automated E2W manufacturing plant in India (by production capacity for an E2W-only OEM, per Redseer Report). It features automated manufacturing with modular and flexible assembly lines and an in-house paint shop.
Context: Pages 162, 165, Our Business
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Ola's EV hub in Krishnagiri and Dharmapuri districts, Tamil Nadu is expected to span up to 2,000 acres. It includes the Ola Futurefactory, the upcoming Ola Gigafactory for cell manufacturing, and co-located suppliers. The State Government of Tamil Nadu has reserved 700 acres for two years for allotment to suppliers co-locating within the EV hub (MoU dated February 18, 2023). Three suppliers were co-located as of DRHP date.
Context: Page 162, Our Business
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Ola manufactures EVs and core EV components (battery packs, motors, vehicle frames) at the Futurefactory. Core components like battery packs, motors, and vehicle frames are shared across S1 Pro, S1 Air, and S1 X+ models. Vehicle frame manufacturing started August 1, 2023 — insourced from third-party suppliers.
Context: Pages 161-162, Our Business
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Pre-IPO capacity utilization: Ola Futurefactory at 1M units/year capacity, but FY23 sales of ~340K units imply ~34% utilization. FY22 sales were much lower, implying ~17% utilization. The fixed cost burden of a 1M-unit factory operating at partial capacity creates per-unit cost pressure that margins must overcome.
Context: Pages 28-31, Risk Factors - operating losses and scale risks
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Material Subsidiary (OET) has opted for Section 115BAB of Income-tax Act providing 15% corporate tax rate for new manufacturing companies. OET imported capital goods without customs duty under EPCG Scheme (export obligation: 6x duty saved within 6 years). OET also claims IGCR benefits for importing goods at concessional customs duty rates.
Context: Pages 131-132, Special Tax Benefits
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Ola and its subsidiaries have entered into an MOU with Government of Tamil Nadu for incentives under the Structured Package of Assistance: (i) 100% SGST reimbursement for 20 years on EVs manufactured, sold and registered in Tamil Nadu, (ii) incentive for GST on capital goods in case of inverted tax situation. Benefits are subject to condition fulfilment; failure requires refund with interest.
